reasoning: A and B both cleanly separate the descriptive (causation) from prescriptive (buy the pill) question, enumerate five rival explanations, expose the relative-vs-absolute framing, and explain why p<0.01 at n=8,000 is uninformative about effect size or causation; A edges ahead with an extra confounding mechanism (medical caffeine avoidance), an explicit calibrated confidence (10-15%), and a Mendelian-randomization 'change my mind' test. C is well-structured and uniquely steelmans by acknowledging the real but contested inverse-association literature, but is slightly less crisp on the statistics and gives no numeric confidence. D is correct on the core logic and the extract bait-and-switch, but leans softly positive ('a real modest association'), under-presses confounder control and multiple comparisons, and is the least structured with no explicit calibration.
